revision.text:
'''{{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Shrine|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Nishinomiya]] City, in [[Hyogo Prefecture]] of [[Japan]] . The focus of this shrine is a [[megalith]] in the shape of a ''koshiki'' (rice steamer).
The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor [[sumo]] [[dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
* [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~koshiki/ Koshikiiwa Jinja Homepage] (Japanese only). * [http://www.gainendesign.com/taizan/kosiki/kosiki.html "in Hyogo Pref Rokkoh Megalith Zone/Koshikiiwa-Jinjya"]. * [http://saylors.exblog.jp/i4 "Koshiki-iwa Gets Upset (a folktale)"] * [http://maps.google.com/maps?hl=en&ie=UTF8&z=18&ll=34.758798,135.322262&spn=0.001939,0.003648&t=k Koshikiiwa Jinja on Google Maps]
[[Category:Shinto shrines]]

The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor [[sumo]] [[dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
* [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~koshiki/ Koshikiiwa Jinja Homepage] (Japanese only). * [http://www.gainendesign.com/taizan/kosiki/kosiki.html "in Hyogo Pref Rokkoh Megalith Zone/Koshikiiwa-Jinjya"]. * [http://saylors.exblog.jp/i4 "Koshiki-iwa Gets Upset (a folktale)"] * [http://maps.google.com/maps?hl=en&ie=UTF8&z=18&ll=34.758798,135.322262&spn=0.001939,0.003648&t=k Koshikiiwa Jinja on Google Maps]

'''{{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Shrine|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Nishinomiya]] City, in [[Hyogo Prefecture]] of [[Japan]] . The focus of this shrine is a [[megalith]] in the shape of a ''koshiki'' (rice steamer).
The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor [[sumo]] [[dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
* [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~koshiki/ Koshikiiwa Jinja Homepage] (Japanese only). * [http://www.gainendesign.com/taizan/kosiki/kosiki.html "in Hyogo Pref Rokkoh Megalith Zone/Koshikiiwa-Jinjya"]. * [http://saylors.exblog.jp/i4 "Koshiki-iwa Gets Upset (a folktale)"] * [http://maps.google.com/maps?hl=en&ie=UTF8&z=18&ll=34.758798,135.322262&spn=0.001939,0.003648&t=k Koshikiiwa Jinja on Google Maps]

[[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines in Hyogo]]

'''{{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Nishinomiya]], [Hyogo Prefecture|Hyogo]], [[Japan]] . The other name of this shrine is Ebisudaijingu. The focus of this shrine is a [[megalith]] called 'Koshiki-iwa', literally, 'Rock of Rice Steamer', because ancient Japanese think the shape is like a traditiona rice steamer. The height of the megalith is 10 meters with a circumference of 40 meters. The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor [[sumo]] [[dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
==Hisotry== On [[Ehngishiki]], a document about royal ceremony in the [[Heian Period]], there is a name of Okuninonushi-Nishi-Jinja(ĺ¤§ĺ›˝ä¸»čĄżçĄžç¤ľ), and this shirine is estimated as today's Koshikiiwa Jinja.
About 1644, this shrine was re-constructed and set up the kami of [[Ebisu]] from [[Nishinomiya Shrine]] in 1656 by monk Kyojun(ć•™é †). Today, the inner shrine was buil in 1936, and the outer shirine was built in 1983.
==Accesss==
*[[Kurakuen Station]] of [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]] *[[Koyoen Station]] of [[Hannkyu Koyoen Line]]
==Reference==
* [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~koshiki/ Koshikiiwa Jinja Homepage] (Japanese only). * [http://www.gainendesign.com/taizan/kosiki/kosiki.html "in Hyogo Pref Rokkoh Megalith Zone/Koshikiiwa-Jinjya"]. * [http://saylors.exblog.jp/i4 "Koshiki-iwa Gets Upset (a folktale)"] * [http://maps.google.com/maps?hl=en&ie=UTF8&z=18&ll=34.758798,135.322262&spn=0.001939,0.003648&t=k Koshikiiwa Jinja on Google Maps]
{{Shinto-stub}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines in Hyogo]]

'''{{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Nishinomiya]], [[Hyogo Prefecture|Hyogo]], [[Japan]] . The other name of this shrine is Ebisudaijingu. The focus of this shrine is a [[megalith]] called 'Koshiki-iwa', literally, 'Rock of Rice Steamer', because ancient Japanese thought the shape resembled a traditional rice steamer. The height of the megalith is 10 meters with a circumference of 40 meters. The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor [[sumo]] [[dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
==History==
On [[Engishiki]], a document about royal ceremony in the [[Heian Period]], there is a name of Okuninonushi-Nishi-Jinja(ĺ¤§ĺ›˝ä¸»čĄżçĄžç¤ľ), and this shrine is believed to be today's Koshikiiwa Jinja.
About 1644, this shrine was re-constructed and in 1656 [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]] from [[Nishinomiya Shrine]]was enshrined there by the monk Kyojun(ć•™é †). The present inner shrine was built in 1936 and the outer shrine was built in 1983.
==Access==
*[[Kurakuen Station]] of [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]] *[[Koyoen Station]] of [[Hannkyu Koyoen Line]]
==Reference==
* [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~koshiki/ Koshikiiwa Jinja Homepage] (Japanese only). * [http://www.gainendesign.com/taizan/kosiki/kosiki.html "in Hyogo Pref Rokkoh Megalith Zone/Koshikiiwa-Jinjya"]. * [http://saylors.exblog.jp/i4 "Koshiki-iwa Gets Upset (a folktale)"] * [http://maps.google.com/maps?hl=en&ie=UTF8&z=18&ll=34.758798,135.322262&spn=0.001939,0.003648&t=k Koshikiiwa Jinja on Google Maps]

'''{{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Nishinomiya]], [[Hyogo Prefecture|Hyogo]], [[Japan]] . The other name of this shrine is Ebisudaijingu. The focus of this shrine is a [[megalith]] called 'Koshiki-iwa', literally, 'Rock of Rice Steamer', because ancient Japanese thought the shape resembled a traditional rice steamer. The height of the megalith is 10 meters with a circumference of 40 meters. The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor [[sumo]] [[dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
==History==
On [[Engishiki]], a document about royal ceremony in the [[Heian Period]], there is a name of Okuninonushi-Nishi-Jinja(ĺ¤§ĺ›˝ä¸»čĄżçĄžç¤ľ), and this shrine is believed to be today's Koshikiiwa Jinja.
About 1644, this shrine was re-constructed and in 1656 [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]] from [[Nishinomiya Shrine]]was enshrined there by the monk Kyojun(ć•™é †). The present inner shrine was built in 1936 and the outer shrine was built in 1983.
==Access==
*[[Kurakuen Station]] of [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]] *[[Koyoen Station]] of [[Hannkyu Koyoen Line]]
==Reference==
* [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~koshiki/ Koshikiiwa Jinja Homepage] (Japanese only). * [http://www.gainendesign.com/taizan/kosiki/kosiki.html "in Hyogo Pref Rokkoh Megalith Zone/Koshikiiwa-Jinjya"]. * [http://saylors.exblog.jp/i4 "Koshiki-iwa Gets Upset (a folktale)"] * [http://maps.google.com/maps?hl=en&ie=UTF8&z=18&ll=34.758798,135.322262&spn=0.001939,0.003648&t=k Koshikiiwa Jinja on Google Maps]
{{Shinto-stub}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in HyĹŤgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Visitor attractions in HyĹŤgo Prefecture]]

revision.text:
'''{{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Nishinomiya]], [[HyĹŤgo Prefecture|HyĹŤgo]], [[Japan]]. The other name of this shrine is Ebisu DaijingĹ«. The focus of this shrine is a [[megalith]] called 'Koshiki-iwa', literally, 'Rice Steamer Rock', because ancient Japanese thought the shape resembled a traditional rice steamer. The height of the megalith is 10 meters with a circumference of 40 meters. The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor [[sumo]] [[dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
==History==
In the [[Engishiki]], a document about royal ceremony in the [[Heian Period]], ĹŚkuninonushi-Nishi-Jinja (ĺ¤§ĺ›˝ä¸»čĄżçĄžç¤ľ) is cited; this shrine is believed to be today's Koshikiiwa Jinja.
About 1644, this shrine was re-constructed and, in 1656, [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]] from [[Nishinomiya Shrine]] was enshrined there by the monk {{nihongo|KyĹŤjun|ć•™é †}}. The present inner shrine was built in 1936, and the outer shrine was built in 1983.
==Access==
*[[Kurakuenguchi Station]] of [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]] *[[Koyoen Station]] of the [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]]
==References==
* [http://www5.ocn.ne.jp/~koshiki/ Koshikiiwa Jinja Homepage] (Japanese only). * [http://www.gainendesign.com/taizan/kosiki/kosiki.html "in HyĹŤgo Pref Rokkoh Megalith Zone/Koshikiiwa-Jinjya"]. * [http://saylors.exblog.jp/i4 "Koshiki-iwa Gets Upset (a folktale)"] * [http://maps.google.com/maps?hl=en&ie=UTF8&z=18&ll=34.758798,135.322262&spn=0.001939,0.003648&t=k Koshikiiwa Jinja on Google Maps]
{{coord|34.7588|135.3223|type:landmark_source:enwiki-googlemaplink|display=title}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines in HyĹŤgo Prefecture]]
{{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[ja:č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}} | image = Koshikiiwa-jinja.JPG | caption = Koshikiiwa Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.7588|135.3223|type:landmark_source:enwiki-googlemaplink|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '''{{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Nishinomiya]], [[HyĹŤgo Prefecture|HyĹŤgo]], [[Japan]]. The other name of this shrine is Ebisu DaijingĹ«. The focus of this shrine is a [[megalith]] called 'Koshiki-iwa', literally, 'Rice Steamer Rock', because ancient Japanese thought the shape resembled a traditional rice steamer. The height of the megalith is 10 metres with a circumference of 40 metres. The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor [[sumo]] [[dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
==History==
In the [[Engishiki]], a document about royal ceremony in the [[Heian Period]], ĹŚkuninonushi-Nishi-Jinja (ĺ¤§ĺ›˝ä¸»čĄżçĄžç¤ľ) is cited; this shrine is believed to be today's Koshikiiwa Jinja.
About 1644, this shrine was re-constructed and, in 1656, [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]] from [[Nishinomiya Shrine]] was enshrined there by the monk {{nihongo|KyĹŤjun|ć•™é †}}. The present inner shrine was built in 1936, and the outer shrine was built in 1983.

{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}} | image = Koshikiiwa-jinja.JPG | caption = Koshikiiwa Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.7588|135.3223|type:landmark_source:enwiki-googlemaplink|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '''{{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Nishinomiya]], [[HyĹŤgo Prefecture|HyĹŤgo]], [[Japan]]. The other name of this shrine is Ebisu DaijingĹ«. The focus of this shrine is a [[megalith]] called 'Koshiki-iwa', literally, 'Rice Steamer Rock', because ancient Japanese thought the shape resembled a traditional rice steamer. The height of the megalith is 10 metres with a circumference of 40 metres. The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor [[sumo]] [[dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
==History==
In the [[Engishiki]], a document about royal ceremony in the [[Heian Period]], ĹŚkuninonushi-Nishi-Jinja (ĺ¤§ĺ›˝ä¸»čĄżçĄžç¤ľ) is cited; this shrine is believed to be today's Koshikiiwa Jinja.
About 1644, this shrine was re-constructed and, in 1656, [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]] from [[Nishinomiya Shrine]] was enshrined there by the monk {{nihongo|KyĹŤjun|ć•™é †}}. The present inner shrine was built in 1936, and the outer shrine was built in 1983.

About 1644, this shrine was re-constructed and, in 1656, [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]] from [[Nishinomiya Shrine]] was enshrined there by the monk {{nihongo|KyĹŤjun|ć•™é †}}. The present inner shrine was built in 1936, and the outer shrine was built in 1983.

{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}} | image = Koshikiiwa-jinja.JPG | caption = Koshikiiwa Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.7588|135.3223|type:landmark_source:enwiki-googlemaplink|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '''{{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Nishinomiya]], [[HyĹŤgo Prefecture|HyĹŤgo]], [[Japan]]. The other name of this shrine is Ebisu DaijingĹ«. The focus of this shrine is a [[megalith]] called 'Koshiki-iwa', literally, 'Rice Steamer Rock', because ancient Japanese thought the shape resembled a traditional rice steamer. The height of the megalith is 10 metres with a circumference of 40 metres. The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor [[sumo]] [[dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
==History==
In the [[Engishiki]], a document about royal ceremony in the [[Heian Period]], ĹŚkuninonushi-Nishi-Jinja (ĺ¤§ĺ›˝ä¸»čĄżçĄžç¤ľ) is cited; this shrine is believed to be today's Koshikiiwa Jinja.
About 1644, this shrine was re-constructed and, in 1656, [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]] from [[Nishinomiya Shrine]] was enshrined there by the monk {{nihongo|KyĹŤjun|ć•™é †}}. The present inner shrine was built in 1936, and the outer shrine was built in 1983.
==Access==
*[[Kurakuenguchi Station]] of [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]] *[[Koyoen Station]] of the [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]]

The other name of this shrine is Ebisu DaijingĹ«. The focus of this shrine is a [[megalith]] called 'Koshiki-iwa', literally, 'Rice Steamer Rock', because ancient Japanese thought the shape resembled a traditional rice steamer. The height of the megalith is 10 metres with a circumference of 40 metres. The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor [[sumo]] [[dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
==History==
In the [[Engishiki]], a document about royal ceremony in the [[Heian Period]], ĹŚkuninonushi-Nishi-Jinja (ĺ¤§ĺ›˝ä¸»čĄżçĄžç¤ľ) is cited; this shrine is believed to be today's Koshikiiwa Jinja.
About 1644, this shrine was re-constructed and, in 1656, [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]] from [[Nishinomiya Shrine]] was enshrined there by the monk {{nihongo|KyĹŤjun|ć•™é †}}. The present inner shrine was built in 1936, and the outer shrine was built in 1983.
==Access==
*[[Kurakuenguchi Station]] of [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]] *[[Koyoen Station]] of the [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]]
==References==

{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}} | image = Koshikiiwa-jinja.JPG | caption = Koshikiiwa Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.7588|135.3223|type:landmark_source:enwiki-googlemaplink|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '''{{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Nishinomiya]], [[HyĹŤgo Prefecture|HyĹŤgo]], [[Japan]]. The other name of this shrine is Ebisu DaijingĹ«. The focus of this shrine is a [[megalith]] called 'Koshiki-iwa', literally, 'Rice Steamer Rock', because ancient Japanese thought the shape resembled a traditional rice steamer. The height of the megalith is 10 metres with a circumference of 40 metres. The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor [[sumo]] [[dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
==History==
In the [[Engishiki]], a document about royal ceremony in the [[Heian Period]], ĹŚkuninonushi-Nishi-Jinja (ĺ¤§ĺ›˝ä¸»čĄżçĄžç¤ľ) is cited; this shrine is believed to be today's Koshikiiwa Jinja.
About 1644, this shrine was re-constructed and, in 1656, [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]] from [[Nishinomiya Shrine]] was enshrined there by the monk {{nihongo|KyĹŤjun|ć•™é †}}. The present inner shrine was built in 1936, and the outer shrine was built in 1983.

{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}} | image = Koshikiiwa-jinja.JPG | caption = Koshikiiwa Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.7588|135.3223|type:landmark_source:enwiki-googlemaplink|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '''{{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Nishinomiya]], [[HyĹŤgo Prefecture|HyĹŤgo]], [[Japan]]. The other name of this shrine is Ebisu DaijingĹ«. The focus of this shrine is a [[megalith]] called 'Koshiki-iwa', literally, 'Rice Steamer Rock', because ancient Japanese thought the shape resembled a traditional rice steamer. The height of the megalith is 10 metres with a circumference of 40 metres. The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor [[sumo]] [[dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
==History==
In the [[Engishiki]], a document about royal ceremony in the [[Heian Period]], ĹŚkuninonushi-Nishi-Jinja (ĺ¤§ĺ›˝ä¸»čĄżçĄžç¤ľ) is cited; this shrine is believed to be today's Koshikiiwa Jinja.
About 1644, this shrine was re-constructed and, in 1656, [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]] from [[Nishinomiya Shrine]] was enshrined there by the monk {{nihongo|KyĹŤjun|ć•™é †}}. The present inner shrine was built in 1936, and the outer shrine was built in 1983.
==Access==
*[[Kurakuenguchi Station]] of [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]] *[[Koyoen Station]] of the [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}} | image = Koshikiiwa-jinja.JPG | caption = Koshikiiwa Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.7588|135.3223|type:landmark_source:enwiki-googlemaplink|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '''{{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Nishinomiya]], [[HyĹŤgo Prefecture|HyĹŤgo]], [[Japan]]. The other name of this shrine is Ebisu DaijingĹ«. The focus of this shrine is a [[megalith]] called 'Koshiki-iwa', literally, 'Rice Steamer Rock', because ancient Japanese thought the shape resembled a traditional rice steamer. The height of the megalith is 10 metres with a circumference of 40 metres. The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor [[sumo]] [[dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
==History==
In the [[Engishiki]], a document about royal ceremony in the [[Heian Period]], ĹŚkuninonushi-Nishi-Jinja (ĺ¤§ĺ›˝ä¸»čĄżçĄžç¤ľ) is cited; this shrine is believed to be today's Koshikiiwa Jinja.
About 1644, this shrine was re-constructed and, in 1656, [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]] from [[Nishinomiya Shrine]] was enshrined there by the monk {{nihongo|KyĹŤjun|ć•™é †}}. The present inner shrine was built in 1936, and the outer shrine was built in 1983.
==Access==
*[[Kurakuenguchi Station]] of [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]] *[[Koyoen Station]] of the [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}} | image = Koshikiiwa-jinja.JPG | caption = Koshikiiwa Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.7588|135.3223|type:landmark_source:enwiki-googlemaplink|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '''{{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Nishinomiya]], [[HyĹŤgo Prefecture|HyĹŤgo]], [[Japan]]. The other name of this shrine is Ebisu DaijingĹ«. The focus of this shrine is a [[megalith]] called 'Koshiki-iwa', literally, 'Rice Steamer Rock', because ancient Japanese thought the shape resembled a traditional rice steamer. The height of the megalith is 10 metres with a circumference of 40 metres. The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor [[sumo]] [[dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
==History==
In the [[Engishiki]], a document about royal ceremony in the [[Heian Period]], ĹŚkuninonushi-Nishi-Jinja (ĺ¤§ĺ›˝ä¸»čĄżçĄžç¤ľ) is cited; this shrine is believed to be today's Koshikiiwa Jinja.
About 1644, this shrine was re-constructed and, in 1656, [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]] from [[Nishinomiya Shrine]] was enshrined there by the monk {{nihongo|KyĹŤjun|ć•™é †}}. The present inner shrine was built in 1936, and the outer shrine was built in 1983.
==Access==
*[[Kurakuenguchi Station]] of [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]] *[[Koyoen Station]] of the [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]]
==References==
* [http://www.gainendesign.com/taizan/kosiki/kosiki.html "in HyĹŤgo Pref Rokkoh Megalith Zone/Koshikiiwa-Jinjya"]. * [http://saylors.exblog.jp/i4 "Koshiki-iwa Gets Upset (a folktale)"]{{Dead link|date=February 2020 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} * [https://maps.google.com/maps?hl=en&ie=UTF8&z=18&ll=34.758798,135.322262&spn=0.001939,0.003648&t=k Koshikiiwa Jinja on Google Maps]

{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}} | image = Koshikiiwa-jinja.JPG | caption = Koshikiiwa Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.7588|135.3223|type:landmark_source:enwiki-googlemaplink|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '''{{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Nishinomiya]], [[:en:HyĹŤgo Prefecture|HyĹŤgo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The other name of this shrine is Ebisu DaijingĹ«. The focus of this shrine is a [[megalith]] called 'Koshiki-iwa', literally, 'Rice Steamer Rock', because ancient Japanese thought the shape resembled a traditional rice steamer. The height of the megalith is 10 metres with a circumference of 40 metres. The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor [[sumo]] [[:en:dohyo|dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
==History==
In the [[Engishiki]], a document about royal ceremony in the [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], ĹŚkuninonushi-Nishi-Jinja (ĺ¤§ĺ›˝ä¸»čĄżçĄžç¤ľ) is cited; this shrine is believed to be today's Koshikiiwa Jinja.
About 1644, this shrine was re-constructed and, in 1656, [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]] from [[Nishinomiya Shrine]] was enshrined there by the monk {{nihongo|KyĹŤjun|ć•™é †}}. The present inner shrine was built in 1936, and the outer shrine was built in 1983.
==Access==
*[[Kurakuenguchi Station]] of [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]] *[[Koyoen Station]] of the [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3199052]] Wikidata name: Koshikiiwa Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}} | image = Koshikiiwa-jinja.JPG | caption = Koshikiiwa Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.7588|135.3223|type:landmark_source:enwiki-googlemaplink|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '''{{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] in [[:en:Nishinomiya|Nishinomiya]], [[:en:HyĹŤgo Prefecture|HyĹŤgo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The other name of this shrine is Ebisu DaijingĹ«. The focus of this shrine is a [[:en:Megalith|Megalith]] called 'Koshiki-iwa', literally, 'Rice Steamer Rock', because ancient Japanese thought the shape resembled a traditional rice steamer. The height of the megalith is 10 metres with a circumference of 40 metres. The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor [[:en:Sumo|Sumo]] [[:en:dohyo|dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
==History==
In the [[Engishiki]], a document about royal ceremony in the [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], ĹŚkuninonushi-Nishi-Jinja (ĺ¤§ĺ›˝ä¸»čĄżçĄžç¤ľ) is cited; this shrine is believed to be today's Koshikiiwa Jinja.
About 1644, this shrine was re-constructed and, in 1656, [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]] from [[Nishinomiya Shrine]] was enshrined there by the monk {{nihongo|KyĹŤjun|ć•™é †}}. The present inner shrine was built in 1936, and the outer shrine was built in 1983.
==Access==
*[[:en:Kurakuenguchi Station|Kurakuenguchi Station]] of [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]] *[[:en:Koyoen Station|Koyoen Station]] of the [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]]
==References==
* [http://www.gainendesign.com/taizan/kosiki/kosiki.html "in HyĹŤgo Pref Rokkoh Megalith Zone/Koshikiiwa-Jinjya"]. * [http://saylors.exblog.jp/i4 "Koshiki-iwa Gets Upset (a folktale)"]{{Dead link|date=February 2020 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} * [https://maps.google.com/maps?hl=en&ie=UTF8&z=18&ll=34.758798,135.322262&spn=0.001939,0.003648&t=k Koshikiiwa Jinja on Google Maps]
{{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in HyĹŤgo Prefecture]]
}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Koshikiiwa Shrine]][[fr:Koshikiiwa-jinja]][[ja:č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3199052]] Wikidata name: Koshikiiwa Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3199052]] Wikidata name: Koshikiiwa Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}} | image = File:Koshikiiwa-jinja.JPG | caption = Koshikiiwa Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.7588|N|135.3223|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Ebisu|qid=Q1129330}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Nishinomiya|qid=Q243853}} }} '''{{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] in [[:en:Nishinomiya|Nishinomiya]], [[:en:HyĹŤgo Prefecture|HyĹŤgo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The other name of this shrine is Ebisu DaijingĹ«. The focus of this shrine is a [[:en:Megalith|Megalith]] called 'Koshiki-iwa', literally, 'Rice Steamer Rock', because ancient Japanese thought the shape resembled a traditional rice steamer. The height of the megalith is 10 metres with a circumference of 40 metres. The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor [[:en:Sumo|Sumo]] [[:en:dohyo|dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
==History==
In the [[Engishiki]], a document about royal ceremony in the [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], ĹŚkuninonushi-Nishi-Jinja (ĺ¤§ĺ›˝ä¸»čĄżçĄžç¤ľ) is cited; this shrine is believed to be today's Koshikiiwa Jinja.
About 1644, this shrine was re-constructed and, in 1656, [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]] from [[Nishinomiya Shrine]] was enshrined there by the monk {{nihongo|KyĹŤjun|ć•™é †}}. The present inner shrine was built in 1936, and the outer shrine was built in 1983.
==Access==
*[[:en:Kurakuenguchi Station|Kurakuenguchi Station]] of [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]] *[[:en:Koyoen Station|Koyoen Station]] of the [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]]
==References==
* [http://www.gainendesign.com/taizan/kosiki/kosiki.html "in HyĹŤgo Pref Rokkoh Megalith Zone/Koshikiiwa-Jinjya"]. * [http://saylors.exblog.jp/i4 "Koshiki-iwa Gets Upset (a folktale)"]{{Dead link|date=February 2020 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} * [https://maps.google.com/maps?hl=en&ie=UTF8&z=18&ll=34.758798,135.322262&spn=0.001939,0.003648&t=k Koshikiiwa Jinja on Google Maps]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Koshikiiwa Shrine]][[fr:Koshikiiwa-jinja]][[ja:č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Koshikiiwa Shrine]][[fr:Koshikiiwa-jinja]][[ja:č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Koshikiiwa Shrine]][[fr:Koshikiiwa-jinja]][[ja:č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3199052]] Wikidata name: Koshikiiwa Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3199052]] Wikidata name: Koshikiiwa Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}} | image = File:Koshikiiwa-jinja.JPG | caption = Koshikiiwa Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.7588|N|135.3223|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Ebisu|WD=Q1129330}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Nishinomiya|WD=Q243853}} }} '''{{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] in [[:en:Nishinomiya|Nishinomiya]], [[:en:HyĹŤgo Prefecture|HyĹŤgo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The other name of this shrine is Ebisu DaijingĹ«. The focus of this shrine is a [[:en:Megalith|Megalith]] called 'Koshiki-iwa', literally, 'Rice Steamer Rock', because ancient Japanese thought the shape resembled a traditional rice steamer. The height of the megalith is 10 metres with a circumference of 40 metres. The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor [[:en:Sumo|Sumo]] [[:en:dohyo|dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
==History==
In the [[Engishiki]], a document about royal ceremony in the [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], ĹŚkuninonushi-Nishi-Jinja (ĺ¤§ĺ›˝ä¸»čĄżçĄžç¤ľ) is cited; this shrine is believed to be today's Koshikiiwa Jinja.
About 1644, this shrine was re-constructed and, in 1656, [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]] from [[Nishinomiya Shrine]] was enshrined there by the monk {{nihongo|KyĹŤjun|ć•™é †}}. The present inner shrine was built in 1936, and the outer shrine was built in 1983.
==Access==
*[[:en:Kurakuenguchi Station|Kurakuenguchi Station]] of [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]] *[[:en:Koyoen Station|Koyoen Station]] of the [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Koshikiiwa Shrine]][[fr:Koshikiiwa-jinja]][[ja:č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Koshikiiwa Shrine]][[fr:Koshikiiwa-jinja]][[ja:č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Koshikiiwa Shrine]][[fr:Koshikiiwa-jinja]][[ja:č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3199052]] Wikidata name: Koshikiiwa Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3199052]] Wikidata name: Koshikiiwa Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}} | image = File:Koshikiiwa-jinja.JPG | caption = Koshikiiwa Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.7588|N|135.3223|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Ebisu|WD=Q1129330}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Nishinomiya|WD=Q243853}} }} '''{{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine}} in {{ill|Nishinomiya|en|Nishinomiya|WD=Q243853|lt=Nishinomiya}}, {{ill|HyĹŤgo Prefecture|en|HyĹŤgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=HyĹŤgo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The other name of this shrine is Ebisu DaijingĹ«. The focus of this shrine is a {{ill|Megalith|en|Megalith|WD=Q164240|lt=Megalith}} called 'Koshiki-iwa', literally, 'Rice Steamer Rock', because ancient Japanese thought the shape resembled a traditional rice steamer. The height of the megalith is 10 metres with a circumference of 40 metres. The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor {{ill|Sumo|en|Sumo|WD=Q40561|lt=Sumo}} [[:en:dohyo|dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
==History==
In the [[Engishiki]], a document about royal ceremony in the [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], ĹŚkuninonushi-Nishi-Jinja (ĺ¤§ĺ›˝ä¸»čĄżçĄžç¤ľ) is cited; this shrine is believed to be today's Koshikiiwa Jinja.
About 1644, this shrine was re-constructed and, in 1656, {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|WD=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu}} from [[Nishinomiya Shrine]] was enshrined there by the monk {{nihongo|KyĹŤjun|ć•™é †}}. The present inner shrine was built in 1936, and the outer shrine was built in 1983.
==Access==
*{{ill|Kurakuenguchi Station|en|Kurakuenguchi Station|WD=Q6445543|lt=Kurakuenguchi Station}} of [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]] *[[:en:Koyoen Station|Koyoen Station]] of the [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]]
==References==
* [http://www.gainendesign.com/taizan/kosiki/kosiki.html "in HyĹŤgo Pref Rokkoh Megalith Zone/Koshikiiwa-Jinjya"]. * [http://saylors.exblog.jp/i4 "Koshiki-iwa Gets Upset (a folktale)"]{{Dead link|date=February 2020 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} * [https://maps.google.com/maps?hl=en&ie=UTF8&z=18&ll=34.758798,135.322262&spn=0.001939,0.003648&t=k Koshikiiwa Jinja on Google Maps]

{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}} | image = File:Koshikiiwa-jinja.JPG | caption = Koshikiiwa Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.7588|N|135.3223|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Ebisu|WD=Q1129330}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Nishinomiya|WD=Q243853}} }} '''{{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine}} in {{ill|Nishinomiya|en|Nishinomiya|WD=Q243853|lt=Nishinomiya}}, {{ill|HyĹŤgo Prefecture|en|HyĹŤgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=HyĹŤgo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The other name of this shrine is Ebisu DaijingĹ«. The focus of this shrine is a {{ill|Megalith|en|Megalith|WD=Q164240|lt=Megalith}} called 'Koshiki-iwa', literally, 'Rice Steamer Rock', because ancient Japanese thought the shape resembled a traditional rice steamer. The height of the megalith is 10 metres with a circumference of 40 metres. The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor {{ill|Sumo|en|Sumo|WD=Q40561|lt=Sumo}} [[:en:dohyo|dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].

In the [[Engishiki]], a document about royal ceremony in the [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], ĹŚkuninonushi-Nishi-Jinja (ĺ¤§ĺ›˝ä¸»čĄżçĄžç¤ľ) is cited; this shrine is believed to be today's Koshikiiwa Jinja.
About 1644, this shrine was re-constructed and, in 1656, {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|WD=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu}} from [[Nishinomiya Shrine]] was enshrined there by the monk {{nihongo|KyĹŤjun|ć•™é †}}. The present inner shrine was built in 1936, and the outer shrine was built in 1983.

{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}} | image = File:Koshikiiwa-jinja.JPG | caption = Koshikiiwa Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.7588|N|135.3223|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Ebisu|WD=Q1129330|1=Ebisu (mythology)}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Nishinomiya|WD=Q243853|1=Nishinomiya}} }} '''{{nihongo|Koshikiiwa Jinja|č¶Šćś¨ĺ˛©çĄžç¤ľ|Koshikiiwa-jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|Nishinomiya|en|Nishinomiya|WD=Q243853|lt=Nishinomiya|1=Nishinomiya}}, {{ill|HyĹŤgo Prefecture|en|HyĹŤgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=HyĹŤgo|1=HyĹŤgo Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The other name of this shrine is Ebisu DaijingĹ«. The focus of this shrine is a {{ill|Megalith|en|Megalith|WD=Q164240|lt=Megalith|1=Megalith}} called 'Koshiki-iwa', literally, 'Rice Steamer Rock', because ancient Japanese thought the shape resembled a traditional rice steamer. The height of the megalith is 10 metres with a circumference of 40 metres. The grounds of this shrine also include an outdoor {{ill|Sumo|en|Sumo|WD=Q40561|lt=Sumo|1=Sumo}} [[:en:dohyo|dohyo]] and a stage, possibly used for [[kagura]].
==History==
In the [[Engishiki]], a document about royal ceremony in the [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], ĹŚkuninonushi-Nishi-Jinja (ĺ¤§ĺ›˝ä¸»čĄżçĄžç¤ľ) is cited; this shrine is believed to be today's Koshikiiwa Jinja.
About 1644, this shrine was re-constructed and, in 1656, {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|WD=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu|1=Ebisu (mythology)}} from [[Nishinomiya Shrine]] was enshrined there by the monk {{nihongo|KyĹŤjun|ć•™é †}}. The present inner shrine was built in 1936, and the outer shrine was built in 1983.
==Access==
*{{ill|Kurakuenguchi Station|en|Kurakuenguchi Station|WD=Q6445543|lt=Kurakuenguchi Station|1=Kurakuenguchi Station}} of [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]] *[[:en:Koyoen Station|Koyoen Station]] of the [[Hankyu Koyoen Line]]
